summary: David Hume views law through the lens of conventionalism, arguing that legal systems evolve to solve coordination problems regarding property. He asserts that "civilized" states are distinguished by their reliance on "general laws"—rules applied consistently to all citizens and officials—and are evaluated by their "convenience" or lack of societal drawbacks.

principles.statement: Wise rulers must balance their states on general laws.
principles.rationale: Laws that are applied consistently to all relevant cases, and to which everyone is subject (including public officials), best coordinate behavior for the benefit of all.

principles.statement: The ultimate object of municipal laws is to settle all questions of justice.
principles.rationale: Legislators should strive for a complete and permanent system of laws to provide stability, regulate society, and resolve disputes.
principles.source_quote: the object of municipal laws is to fix all the questions with regard to justice.

principles.statement: Laws are conventions that evolve to protect property through mutual coordination.
principles.rationale: People recognize it is in their interest to follow rules protecting property, provided they see that everyone else follows these rules as well.

mental_models.name: Civilized vs. Barbarous Government
mental_models.description: A lens for evaluating the maturity of a state's legal system. "Civilized" governments are characterized by their ability to implement and adhere to "general laws" (consistent rules applying to everyone, including officials), whereas "barbarous" governments lack this consistency and universal application.

mental_models.name: Legal Conventionalism
mental_models.description: Viewing laws not as divine mandates or absolute moral truths, but as evolved solutions to coordination problems (specifically regarding property) that persist because of mutual benefit.

signature_quotes.text: the object of municipal laws is to fix all the questions with regard to justice.
signature_quotes.context: Hume describing the ideal goal of a legislator creating a complete system of laws.

signature_quotes.text: what general laws are, upon the whole, attended with fewest inconveniencies.
signature_quotes.context: Hume describing the standard by which different systems of equivalently general laws should be judged.

anti_patterns.description: Believing that civil laws must meet some prior "natural law" standard to be legitimately called laws.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Laws are actually conventions that evolve through trial and error to solve coordination problems, rather than reflections of an absolute prior moral standard.

anti_patterns.description: Searching for "general laws" in physics and the study of nature.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It is a misguided search for causal principles that we mistakenly believe we can know with absolute certainty.
